ADD S1: Scope: The division and the U.S. DOJ, Treasury, and federal banking regulators have roles in implementing U.S. sanctions programs relevant to financial institutions.
ADD S2: Problems: Financial institutions have been assessed over $12 billion in fines, penalties, and forfeitures for violations of BSA/AML, FCPA, and U.S. sanctions program requirements.
ADD S3: Key Findings: From January 2009 through December 2015, financial institutions were assessed $5.2 billion for BSA violations, $27 million for FCPA violations, and $6.8 billion for U.S. sanctions program requirements.
ADD S4: Key Findings: From January 2009 to December 2015, DOJ, FinCEN, and federal financial regulators assessed and collected about $5.2 billion and $5.1 billion in penalties, fines, and forfeitures for various BSA violations.
ADD S5: Key Findings: OFAC assessed $301 million in penalties against financial institutions for sanctions programs violations from January 2009 through December 2015.
ADD S6: Key Findings: The Federal Reserve independently assessed and collected about $837 million in penalties from six financial institutions for U.S. sanctions programs violations from January 2009 through December 2015.
ADD S7: Key Findings: From January 2009 through December 2015, about $2.7 billion was collected from financial institutions for the covered violations and deposited into Treasury General Fund accounts.
ADD S8: Recommendations: U.S. DOJ had not brought any criminal cases against financial institutions for violations of FCPA.

ADD S1: The division and the 93 U.S. DOJ also has criminal enforcement authority. Treasury, DOJ, and federal banking regulators all have roles in implementing U.S. sanctions programs requirements relevant to financial institutions.
ADD S2: Since 2009, financial institutions have been assessed about $12 billion in fines, penalties, and forfeitures for violations of BSA/AML, FCPA, and U.S. sanctions program requirements.
ADD S3: Specifically, from January 2009 through December 2015, federal agencies assessed about $5.2 billion for BSA violations, $27 million for FCPA violations, and about $6.8 billion for violations of U.S. sanctions program requirements.
ADD S4: From January 2009 to December 2015, DOJ, FinCEN, and federal financial regulators (the Federal Reserve, FDIC, OCC, and SEC), assessed about $5.2 billion and collected about $5.1 billion in penalties, fines, and forfeitures for various BSA violations.
ADD S5: Out of the $1.4 billion assessed, $651 million was assessed concurrently with FinCEN to 13 different financial institutions.
ADD S6: As of December 2015, SEC had collected about $9.4 million of the $16 million it has assessed.
ADD S7: From January 2009 through December 2015, OFAC independently assessed $301 million in penalties against financial institutions for sanctions programs violations.
ADD S8: From January 2009 through December 2015, the Federal Reserve independently assessed and collected about $837 million in penalties from six financial institutions for U.S. sanctions programs violations.
ADD S9: From January 2009 through December 2015, about $2.7 billion was collected from financial institutions for the covered violations and deposited into Treasury General Fund accounts.
ADD S10: FinCEN and financial regulators deposit collections of penalties assessed against financial institutions—including for the covered violations—into Treasury’s General Fund accounts (see fig. FinCEN deposits the penalty payments it receives in accounts in Treasury’s General Fund.
REPLACE S10: U.S. DOJ had not brought any criminal cases against financial institutions for violations of FCPA.

REPLACE S3: Key Findings: From January 2009 through December 2015, federal agencies assessed $5.2 billion for BSA violations, $27 million for FCPA violations, and $6.8 billion for U.S. sanctions program violations. DOJ, FinCEN, and federal financial regulators assessed $5.2 billion and collected $5.1 billion in penalties, fines, and forfeitures for BSA violations. SEC collected $9.4 million of $16 million assessed for FCPA violations. OFAC assessed $301 million in penalties for sanctions program violations.

ADD S2: Problems Identified: Financial institutions have faced significant fines, penalties, and forfeitures for violations of BSA/AML, FCPA, and U.S. sanctions program requirements.
REPLACE S3: Key Findings: From January 2009 through December 2015, financial institutions were assessed $12 billion in fines, penalties, and forfeitures. Specifically, $5.2 billion were for BSA violations, $27 million for FCPA violations, and $6.8 billion for U.S. sanctions program violations. DOJ, FinCEN, and federal financial regulators assessed $5.2 billion and collected $5.1 billion. SEC collected $9.4 million of $16 million assessed. OFAC assessed $301 million in penalties, with $299 million collected.
REPLACE S4: Recommendations: Recommendations include enhancing compliance programs, increasing oversight, and improving enforcement mechanisms to prevent future violations.

ADD S3: From January 2009 to December 2015, DOJ, FinCEN, and federal financial regulators assessed about $5.2 billion and collected about $5.1 billion in penalties, fines, and forfeitures for various BSA violations. Financial regulators assessed a total of about $1.4 billion in penalties for BSA violations for which they were responsible for collecting, and collected almost all of this amount (see fig. Of the $1.4 billion, one penalty (assessed by OCC) accounted for almost 35 percent ($500 million). Financial regulators assess penalties for BSA violations both independently and concurrently with FinCEN. Out of the $1.4 billion assessed, $651 million was assessed concurrently with FinCEN to 13 different financial institutions. SEC’s penalties ranged from $25,000 to $10 million—which included a $4.2 million disgorgement. As of December 2015, SEC had collected about $9.4 million of the $16 million it has assessed. In addition, FinCEN assessed about $108 million in penalties that it was responsible for collecting. Based on our analysis, almost all of the $108 million was assessed in 2015 of which $9.5 million has been collected as of December 2015. Almost all of this amount resulted from forfeitures, while about $1 million was from fines. As of December 2015, $1.2 million had not been collected in the cases we reviewed. DOJ’s forfeitures ranged from about $240,000 to $1.7 billion, and six of the forfeitures were at least $100 million. From January 2009 through December 2015, SEC collected approximately $27 million in penalties and disgorgements from two financial institutions for FCPA violations. SEC assessed $10.3 million in penalties, $13.6 million in disgorgements, and $3.3 million in interest combined for the FCPA violations. From January 2009 through December 2015, OFAC independently assessed $301 million in penalties against financial institutions for sanctions programs violations. The $301 million OFAC assessed was comprised of 47 penalties, with penalty amounts ranging from about $8,700 to $152 million. Of the $301 million, OFAC has collected about $299 million (see fig.
REPLACE S14: Key Findings: The key findings include $5.2 billion in BSA violations, $27 million in FCPA violations, and $301 million in U.S. sanctions program violations, highlighting the substantial financial impact on financial institutions.
REPLACE S9: U.S. Sanctions Program Violations: $301 million in U.S. sanctions program violations.
REPLACE S4: Recommendations: The recommendations suggest strategies for reducing penalties and improving compliance, focusing on better regulatory oversight and financial institution preparedness, and addressing the specific issues highlighted in the key findings.

ADD S3: Agencies involved: Federal Reserve, FDIC, OCC, SEC, FinCEN, NCUA, DOJ, OFAC.
REPLACE S1: Scope: The report analyzes enforcement actions taken against financial institutions for violations of BSA/AML, FCPA, and U.S. sanctions programs from January 2009 through December 2015.
REPLACE S2: Problems Identified: Financial institutions faced significant penalties for BSA/AML, FCPA, and sanctions program violations, impacting their financial health and compliance efforts.
REPLACE S3: Key Findings: BSA/AML violations: $1.4 billion, FCPA violations: $10.3 million, U.S. sanctions program violations: $301 million.
REPLACE S4: Recommendations: Improve regulatory oversight and financial institution preparedness to reduce penalties and enhance compliance.
REPLACE S5: Agencies: Federal Reserve, FDIC, OCC, SEC, FinCEN, NCUA, DOJ, OFAC.
REPLACE S6: Penalties, fines, and forfeitures: Total of $12 billion, with significant contributions from BSA/AML, FCPA, and sanctions program violations.
REPLACE S7: BSA/AML violations: Significant penalties assessed by financial regulators, totaling $1.4 billion, with $651 million assessed concurrently with FinCEN to 13 institutions.
REPLACE S8: FCPA violations: SEC assessed $10.3 million in penalties and $13.6 million in disgorgements from two financial institutions.
REPLACE S9: U.S. sanctions program violations: OFAC assessed $301 million in penalties against financial institutions, with $299 million collected.
REPLACE S19: Financial regulators, including DOJ, FinCEN, and federal banking regulators, assessed and collected significant penalties, fines, and forfeitures for BSA/AML, FCPA, and sanctions program violations, impacting financial institutions' financial health and compliance efforts.

ADD S3: Agencies involved: Federal Reserve, FDIC, OCC, SEC, FinCEN, NCUA, DOJ, OFAC.
REPLACE S1: Scope: The report analyzes enforcement actions taken by various agencies against financial institutions for violations of BSA/AML, FCPA, and U.S. sanctions programs from January 2009 through December 2015.
REPLACE S2: Problems Identified: Financial institutions faced substantial penalties for BSA/AML, FCPA, and sanctions program violations, impacting their financial health and compliance efforts.
REPLACE S4: Recommendations: Improve regulatory oversight, enhance financial institution preparedness, and address specific BSA, FCPA, and sanctions program violations.
REPLACE S5: Penalties, fines, and forfeitures: Total of $12 billion, with significant contributions from BSA, FCPA, and sanctions program violations.
REPLACE S6: BSA violations: $1.4 billion, FCPA violations: $10.3 million, U.S. sanctions program violations: $301 million.
REPLACE S7: FCPA violations: SEC assessed $10.3 million in penalties and $13.6 million in disgorgements from two financial institutions.
REPLACE S8: U.S. sanctions program violations: OFAC assessed $301 million in penalties against financial institutions, with $299 million collected.
REPLACE S9: Financial regulators, including DOJ, FinCEN, and federal banking regulators, assessed and collected significant penalties, fines, and forfeitures for BSA, FCPA, and sanctions program violations, impacting financial institutions' financial health and compliance efforts.
REPLACE S19: Financial regulators, including DOJ, FinCEN, and federal banking regulators, assessed and collected significant penalties, fines, and forfeitures for BSA, FCPA, and sanctions program violations, impacting financial institutions' financial health and compliance efforts.

ADD END: OF the $301 million, OFAC has collected about $299 million (see fig. 3). OFAC’s enforcement guidelines provide the legal framework for analyzing apparent violations. Some of the factors which determine the size of a civil money penalty include the sanctions program at issue and the number of apparent violations and their value. For example, OFAC assessed Clearstream Banking a $152 million penalty because it made securities transfers for the central bank of a sanctioned country. DOJ, along with participating Treasury offices and other law enforcement partners, assessed and enforced criminal and civil forfeitures and fines totaling about $5.7 billion for the federal government for sanctions programs violations. This amount was the result of eight forfeitures that also included two fines. Of the $5.7 billion collected for sanctions programs violations, most of this amount was collected from one financial institution—BNP Paribas. In total, BNP Paribas was assessed an $8.8 billion forfeiture and a $140 million criminal fine in 2014 for willfully conspiring to commit violations of various sanctions laws and regulations. BNP Paribas pleaded guilty to moving more than $8.8 billion through the U.S. financial system on behalf of sanctioned entities from 2004 to 2012. Of the $8.8 billion forfeited, $3.8 billion was collected by Treasury’s Executive Office for Asset Forfeiture, with the remainder apportioned among participating state and local agencies. In addition to BNP Paribas, DOJ and OFAC assessed fines and forfeitures against other financial institutions for similar violations, including processing transactions in violation of the International Emergency Economic Powers Act, OFAC regulations, and the Trading with the Enemy Act. From January 2009 through December 2015, the Federal Reserve independently assessed and collected about $837 million in penalties from six financial institutions for U.S. sanctions programs violations. The Federal Reserve assessed its largest penalty for $508 million against BNP Paribas for having unsafe and unsound practices that failed to prevent the concealing of payment information of financial institutions subject to OFAC regulations. It was assessed as part of a global settlement with DOJ for concealing payment information of a financial institution subject to OFAC regulations. Federal Reserve officials stated that the remaining assessed penalties related to OFAC regulations were largely for similar unsafe and unsound practices. FinCEN and financial regulators have processes in place for receiving penalty payments from financial institutions—including for penalties assessed for the covered violations—and for depositing these payments.
REPLACE S6: Penalties, fines, and forfeitures: Total of $12.5 billion, with significant contributions from BSA, FCPA, and sanctions program violations.
REPLACE S19: OF the $301 million, OFAC has collected about $299 million (see fig. 3). OFAC’s enforcement guidelines provide the legal framework for analyzing apparent violations. Some of the factors which determine the size of a civil money penalty include the sanctions program at issue and the number of apparent violations and their value. For example, OFAC assessed Clearstream Banking a $152 million penalty because it made securities transfers for the central bank of a sanctioned country.
